stimulus: Drumming is a coordinated alarm behavior that researchers have recorded in colonies of leaf-cutter ants. When drumming, sentry ants strike the ground rapidly with their abdomens, producing a sharp vibration that runs through the nest. The signal is set off when army ants begin scouting near a colony, and it warns nestmates to retreat before being raided. Researchers suggest that the behavior is a specialized warning aimed at army ants, since the ants do not drum when other, larger predators approach the colony.
stem: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

answerOptions.content: Several species of ant are known to use drumming as a way of defending against army ants.
Researchers are not sure how leaf-cutter ants defend against predators that are larger than army ants.
Researchers think that drumming in leaf-cutter ants is a specialized warning aimed at army ants.
Army ants are known to be the most common predator of leaf-cutter ant colonies.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer. The text describes drumming as a coordinated alarm behavior and explicitly states that researchers think it is a specialized warning aimed at army ants because the ants do not drum in response to other predators.

Choice A is incorrect; the text discusses only leaf-cutter ants, not other ant species. Choice B is incorrect; the text only notes that drumming isn't used against other predators, not that researchers are uncertain about defenses against them. Choice D is incorrect; the text says army ants are one predator but never claims they are the most common.
